data.title: Primo Global Holdings (TSE:367A) Margin Improvement And 57.3% Earnings Growth Challenge Bearish Narratives
data.description:
Primo Global Holdings (TSE:367A) has just posted another solid set of numbers, with Q1 2026 revenue at ¥7.7b and net income of ¥797m, translating into basic EPS of ¥91.12, while trailing 12 month EPS reached ¥235.39 on revenue of ¥28.96b and net income of ¥2.06b, alongside reported earnings growth of 57.3% over the past year. Over recent quarters the company has seen revenue move from ¥6.70b and EPS of ¥59.91 in Q1 2025 to ¥7.53b with EPS of ¥16.46 in Q4 2025 and then to ¥7.07b with EPS of ¥67.91 in Q3 2025. This feeds into a trailing net profit margin of 7.1% compared with 5.1% a year earlier. For investors, that margin profile and earnings trajectory frame this result as a story about how efficiently the business is converting a steady revenue base into profit.

See our full analysis for Primo Global Holdings.

Next up is how these earnings stack against the prevailing narratives around Primo Global Holdings, highlighting where the story in the numbers lines up with expectations and where it may prompt a rethink.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

57.3% earnings growth backs higher 7.1% margin

  • Over the last 12 months, earnings grew 57.3% and net profit margin sat at 7.1% compared with 5.1% a year earlier, pointing to more profit being kept from roughly the same revenue base.
  • What stands out for a more bullish take is how this profitability story lines up with Primo Global being a focused bridal jewelry retailer across Asia, as earnings quality is described as high even while the business remains concentrated in one product category and relies on store networks in markets like Japan and Mainland China.
    • Supporters of a bullish angle can point to trailing 12 month net income of ¥2,059m on revenue of ¥28,957m, which is a higher profit level than the ¥1,150m earned on ¥24,900m two years earlier.
    • At the same time, critics of the bullish view may focus on the fact that revenue figures are not broken out quarter by quarter beyond the basic totals, so it is harder to separate pure margin improvement from any underlying revenue trend.

Investors who want to see how these profit trends fit into the wider company story can tap into community views through the Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives.

P/E of 11.1x versus peers at 21.3x

  • The shares trade on a trailing P/E of 11.1x, which sits below both the peer average of 21.3x and the JP Specialty Retail industry average of 14.2x, while the current share price is ¥2,612.
  • Bears argue that a lower P/E can sometimes signal market caution about the business, yet the recent 57.3% earnings growth and 7.1% margin pose questions about whether that discount is fully explained by fundamentals.
    • The trailing 12 month EPS of ¥235.39, built from quarterly contributions such as ¥91.12 in Q1 2026 and ¥67.91 in Q3 2025, shows that the company is currently producing earnings that support the P/E level based on the ¥2,612 share price.
    • On the other hand, the high level of debt highlighted in the risk summary gives bears a concrete factor to point to when explaining why the market might keep the P/E below peers, even with stronger recent profit numbers.

DCF fair value ¥8,000.91 versus price of ¥2,612

  • The DCF fair value is set at ¥8,000.91 per share compared with a market price of ¥2,612, which means the shares are trading about 67.4% below that modelled value.
  • Supporters of a bullish narrative see this gap, alongside the 57.3% earnings growth and 7.1% margin, as heavily supporting the idea that the market could be underpricing the company, while the identified high debt level is the main counterpoint that keeps this from looking like a simple valuation story.
    • The trailing 12 month net income of ¥2,059m against revenue of ¥28,957m provides the cash generation backdrop used in a DCF framework, which is what leads to the ¥8,000.91 fair value estimate.
    • At the same time, the P/E of 11.1x versus an industry average of 14.2x shows that even on a simpler valuation yardstick, the shares sit at a discount that bullish investors may compare with the much higher DCF output.

data.title: Neo Performance Materials Advances European Rare Earth Magnet Supply Chain
data.description:
• Neo Performance Materials (TSX:NEO) has commissioned a heavy rare earth element solvent extraction line at its Silmet facility in Estonia.
  • The company has produced its first separated terbium and dysprosium solutions, establishing new rare earth separation capability in Europe.

Neo Performance Materials, listed on the TSX under ticker NEO, focuses on advanced materials, including rare earth processing and magnet manufacturing. The new heavy rare earth line at Silmet fits with growing European interest in securing access to critical inputs for electric vehicles, wind turbines, robotics and automation. For investors, this move connects directly to policy and industry efforts to localise key parts of the supply chain.

The commissioning and initial output from the new line also sit alongside progress at Neo's magnet manufacturing facility in Estonia, which could create a more integrated rare earth value chain in one region. Readers watching TSX:NEO may want to track how the company links separated terbium and dysprosium supply with downstream magnet production and potential customer relationships in Europe.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Neo Performance Materials by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Neo Performance Materials.

📰 Beyond the headline: 2 risks and 2 things going right for Neo Performance Materials that every investor should see.

This commissioning moves Neo a step further along its plan to build a Europe-based rare earth magnet supply chain. Producing separated terbium and dysprosium solutions at nameplate capacity is important because these heavy rare earths are often seen as a bottleneck in high-performance magnet production. For investors, the key point is that the processing is done entirely in Europe, which aligns with EU Critical Raw Materials objectives and could matter to automakers and industrial customers that care about local content and traceability. The Silmet line also feeds directly into Neo's magnet facility in Estonia, so the company is not just selling raw materials, it is trying to connect upstream separation with downstream magnet output and potential long-term supply contracts.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Neo Performance Materials Narrative

  • The Silmet heavy rare earth line supports the narrative that Neo is building a rare earth magnet bottleneck in Europe by pairing material separation with planned magnet capacity for EVs, wind and industrial uses.
  • At the same time, running a technically complex solvent extraction line continuously adds operational execution risk, which the narrative already highlights as a concern for multi-year ramp plans.
  • The commissioning of heavy rare earth separation capacity is quite specific, and this level of upstream processing detail may not be fully captured in broader narrative references to localized supply chains and higher margin downstream products.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Neo Performance Materials to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Heavy rare earth solvent extraction is technically demanding, so any purity, throughput or cost issues could affect margins and slow the move toward routine production.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged 2 key risks for Neo, including pressure on dividend coverage and the possibility that shifts in demand or input pricing could weigh on earnings.
  • 🎁 The new European separation capability may make Neo more relevant to customers prioritizing secure, local supply of terbium and dysprosium for high-performance magnets.
  • 🎁 Analysts have also highlighted 2 rewards, such as growth expectations and the gap to analyst price targets, which many investors will weigh alongside this new operational milestone.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, watch how quickly Neo moves from nameplate commissioning to stable, routine production at Silmet, and whether the company starts to reference specific offtake agreements or customer qualification wins tied to terbium and dysprosium. It also helps to monitor how this separation capability links to ramp-up at the Estonian magnet facility and whether management comments on utilisation or contract visibility across the combined value chain. In the wider rare earth space, investors may compare Neo's progress with other processors and magnet players such as Lynas, MP Materials or producers that work closely with European OEMs, to gauge how differentiated this fully European flow sheet really is.

data.title: Is It Time To Reassess Acushnet Holdings (GOLF) After Its 64% One-Year Rally?
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Acushnet Holdings at around US$96 per share still offers value, the key is to look past the headline returns and examine what the current price implies.
  • The stock has seen a 2.1% decline over the last 7 days, a 2.0% return over 30 days, a 17.4% return year to date, and a 64.5% return over the past year. This can change how the market is pricing its future prospects and risks.
  • Recent attention on Acushnet Holdings has focused on its position in the golf equipment and apparel market and how investors are reacting to that story. This context helps explain why the share price has moved the way it has over different time frames.
  • Even so, Acushnet Holdings currently holds a valuation score of 2 out of 6. The next step is to compare what traditional valuation approaches say about the stock and then look at an even broader way to think about value later in the article.

Acushnet Holdings scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Acushnet Holdings Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes the cash Acushnet Holdings is expected to generate in the future and discounts each year’s figure back to today using a required rate of return. The sum of those discounted cash flows is used as an estimate of what the entire business may be worth right now.

For Acushnet Holdings, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow is about $116.8 million, and analysts provide explicit forecasts out to 2027, with Simply Wall St extrapolating cash flows further out to 2035. Within these projections, forecast Free Cash Flow for 2027 is $241.9 million, and the 10 year path includes discounted values that stay in the low $200 million range each year based on the supplied table.

Putting all of those discounted cash flows together gives an estimated intrinsic value of about $113.94 per share. Compared with the current share price of around $96, the model suggests Acushnet Holdings trades at roughly a 15.4% discount, which points to the shares looking undervalued on this specific cash flow view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Acushnet Holdings.

Approach 2: Acushnet Holdings Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Acushnet Holdings, the P/E ratio is a practical way to judge how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. Investors usually accept a higher or lower P/E depending on what they expect for future growth and how much risk they see in the business, so there is no single "right" number that fits every company.

Acushnet Holdings currently trades on a P/E of 29.9x. That sits well above the Leisure industry average P/E of about 18.5x and also below the peer group average of 38.7x, so the stock is priced between the broader industry and closer peers. To refine this further, Simply Wall St calculates a proprietary "Fair Ratio" of 18.4x, which is the P/E level suggested after factoring in elements such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and specific risks.

This Fair Ratio aims to be more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it ties the multiple to Acushnet Holdings own characteristics instead of broad group averages. Comparing the current P/E of 29.9x with the Fair Ratio of 18.4x suggests the shares are trading above that model derived level.

Result: OVERVALUED

This is why different investors can reasonably land on very different fair values, such as a more optimistic US$110 price target that leans on solid demand, premium products and capital returns, or a more cautious US$95 view that focuses on tariff pressures, competition and the risk that current expectations may be too high.

data.title: Is It Too Late To Consider Revolution Medicines (RVMD) After A 321.4% One Year Surge?
data.description:
• Wondering if Revolution Medicines at around US$152.54 is priced for perfection or still leaves room for value? This article looks at what the current market price may be implying.
  • The stock has seen sharp moves recently, with returns of 57.7% over 7 days, 54.1% over 30 days, 93.0% year to date and 321.4% over the past year. These moves can reshape how investors think about both upside and risk.
  • Recent attention around Revolution Medicines has focused on its position in the pharmaceuticals and biotech space and how investors are reacting to its progress and potential within that sector. That backdrop helps explain why such strong recent returns are drawing in new interest as well as closer scrutiny of the price.
  • Even with this excitement, the company currently holds a valuation score of 2 out of 6. Next up is a look at standard valuation approaches and how they compare with a more complete way to think about value later in the article.

Revolution Medicines scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Revolution Medicines Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to a present value using a required rate of return.

For Revolution Medicines, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow stands at a loss of about US$913.7 million. Analyst and extrapolated projections in the model show free cash flow moving from losses in the coming years to a projected US$1,248.1 million in 2030, with further estimates extending out to 2035 based on those earlier inputs.

Putting all of those projected cash flows together, Simply Wall St’s DCF output arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about US$509.01 per share. Compared with the current share price of around US$152.54, this implies the shares are about 70.0% below that modelled value. Within this framework, the stock is treated as undervalued.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Revolution Medicines is undervalued by 70.0%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 60 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Revolution Medicines.

Approach 2: Revolution Medicines Price vs Book

For companies that are still loss making or reinvesting heavily, earnings based metrics such as the P/E ratio can be less helpful. In those cases, balance sheet based measures like the price to book, or P/B, ratio are often used instead, because they compare the share price with the accounting value of the company’s net assets.

Growth expectations and risk still matter for P/B. A higher growth outlook or stronger perceived competitive position can justify investors paying a higher multiple of book value, while higher risk, uncertainty or weaker profitability usually point to a lower, more conservative multiple as a rough guide to what might be considered “normal” for a stock.

Revolution Medicines currently trades at a P/B of 18.53x. That sits well above the Biotechs industry average of about 2.37x and also above the peer group average of 9.53x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio is a proprietary estimate of what a suitable P/B might be once factors such as earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks are considered together. Because it aims to tailor the multiple to the company’s own profile instead of relying only on broad peer or industry comparisons, it can give a more tailored view of whether the current P/B looks stretched or conservative. In this case, with no separate Fair Ratio provided to compare against the actual 18.53x, the multiple simply looks high relative to both sector and peers without a clear model based anchor.

Result: OVERVALUED

P/B ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 19 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Revolution Medicines Narrative

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. Narratives on Simply Wall St let you attach your own story about Revolution Medicines to the numbers by linking what you believe about its RAS focused cancer pipeline, future revenue, earnings and margins to a financial forecast and a Fair Value. You can then compare this with the current price, with the platform continuously updating those Narratives as new earnings or trial news arrives. This lets you see, for example, how one investor might build a bullish Narrative around a Fair Value near US$170.0, while another takes a more cautious view closer to US$73.0, and decide for yourself which version of the story feels closer to what you think is realistic.

For Revolution Medicines however we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Revolution Medicines Narratives:

🐂 Revolution Medicines Bull Case

Fair Value: US$170.00

Implied discount to this Narrative: about 10.3% below that fair value at the recent US$152.54 share price

Revenue growth assumption: 1,184.41%

  • Focuses on a RAS oncology story where eight Phase III programs, multiple FDA designations and combination regimens could support sizeable future revenue and earnings if trials and adoption progress as analysts expect.
  • Builds in bullish assumptions that revenue reaches US$2.1b by 2029 and earnings move to US$424.4m. The shares are assumed to trade on a P/E of 117.4x, with a 7.1% discount rate used to bring those numbers back to today.
  • Highlights meaningful risks around trial outcomes, concentration in RAS cancers, execution across many global studies and commercial build out that could challenge those optimistic assumptions.

🐻 Revolution Medicines Bear Case

Fair Value: US$133.70

Implied premium to this Narrative: about 14.1% above that fair value at the recent US$152.54 share price

Revenue growth assumption: 902.29%

  • Frames Revolution Medicines as a late stage, RAS focused oncology company with eight Phase III trials and broad partnerships, but stresses that it still reports no revenue while running high operating expenses.
  • Assumes revenue of US$1.0b by 2029 and earnings of US$148.6m, with a profit margin moving toward the US Biotechs industry average. This would imply a very high P/E of 263.8x on those earnings using a 7.1% discount rate.
  • Flags concentration risk in RAS programs, execution and adoption challenges and future financing and royalty obligations as key reasons why the current price could sit ahead of this more conservative fair value view.

data.title: Investors Can Find Comfort In Paradox Interactive's (STO:PDX) Earnings Quality
data.description:
Soft earnings didn't appear to concern Paradox Interactive AB (publ)'s (STO:PDX) shareholders over the last week. Our analysis suggests that while the profits are soft, the foundations of the business are strong.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

A Closer Look At Paradox Interactive's Earnings

In high finance, the key ratio used to measure how well a company converts reported profits into free cash flow (FCF) is the accrual ratio (from cashflow). To get the accrual ratio we first subtract FCF from profit for a period, and then divide that number by the average operating assets for the period. You could think of the accrual ratio from cashflow as the 'non-FCF profit ratio'.

Therefore, it's actually considered a good thing when a company has a negative accrual ratio, but a bad thing if its accrual ratio is positive. That is not intended to imply we should worry about a positive accrual ratio, but it's worth noting where the accrual ratio is rather high. Notably, there is some academic evidence that suggests that a high accrual ratio is a bad sign for near-term profits, generally speaking.

Paradox Interactive has an accrual ratio of -0.36 for the year to December 2025. That implies it has very good cash conversion, and that its earnings in the last year actually significantly understate its free cash flow. Indeed, in the last twelve months it reported free cash flow of kr581m, well over the kr125.5m it reported in profit. Paradox Interactive shareholders are no doubt pleased that free cash flow improved over the last twelve months. However, that's not all there is to consider. We can see that unusual items have impacted its statutory profit, and therefore the accrual ratio.

See our latest analysis for Paradox Interactive

That might leave you wondering what analysts are forecasting in terms of future profitability. Luckily, you can click here to see an interactive graph depicting future profitability, based on their estimates.

The Impact Of Unusual Items On Profit

Paradox Interactive's profit was reduced by unusual items worth kr355m in the last twelve months, and this helped it produce high cash conversion, as reflected by its unusual items. In a scenario where those unusual items included non-cash charges, we'd expect to see a strong accrual ratio, which is exactly what has happened in this case. It's never great to see unusual items costing the company profits, but on the upside, things might improve sooner rather than later. When we analysed the vast majority of listed companies worldwide, we found that significant unusual items are often not repeated. And, after all, that's exactly what the accounting terminology implies. Paradox Interactive took a rather significant hit from unusual items in the year to December 2025. As a result, we can surmise that the unusual items made its statutory profit significantly weaker than it would otherwise be.

Our Take On Paradox Interactive's Profit Performance

Considering both Paradox Interactive's accrual ratio and its unusual items, we think its statutory earnings are unlikely to exaggerate the company's underlying earnings power. After considering all this, we reckon Paradox Interactive's statutory profit probably understates its earnings potential! data.title: We Don’t Think Zedcor's (CVE:ZDC) Earnings Should Make Shareholders Too Comfortable
data.description:
Zedcor Inc.'s (CVE:ZDC) stock performed strongly after the recent earnings report. Despite this, we feel that there are some reasons to be cautious with these earnings.

Trump has pledged to "unleash" American oil and gas and these 15 US stocks have developments that are poised to benefit.

Advertisement

Examining Cashflow Against Zedcor's Earnings

One key financial ratio used to measure how well a company converts its profit to free cash flow (FCF) is the accrual ratio. The accrual ratio subtracts the FCF from the profit for a given period, and divides the result by the average operating assets of the company over that time. This ratio tells us how much of a company's profit is not backed by free cashflow.

Therefore, it's actually considered a good thing when a company has a negative accrual ratio, but a bad thing if its accrual ratio is positive. While having an accrual ratio above zero is of little concern, we do think it's worth noting when a company has a relatively high accrual ratio. To quote a 2014 paper by Lewellen and Resutek, "firms with higher accruals tend to be less profitable in the future".

Zedcor has an accrual ratio of 0.70 for the year to December 2025. As a general rule, that bodes poorly for future profitability. To wit, the company did not generate one whit of free cashflow in that time. In the last twelve months it actually had negative free cash flow, with an outflow of CA$49m despite its profit of CA$2.72m, mentioned above. Coming off the back of negative free cash flow last year, we imagine some shareholders might wonder if its cash burn of CA$49m, this year, indicates high risk. Unfortunately for shareholders, the company has also been issuing new shares, diluting their share of future earnings.

That might leave you wondering what analysts are forecasting in terms of future profitability. Luckily, you can click here to see an interactive graph depicting future profitability, based on their estimates.

To understand the value of a company's earnings growth, it is imperative to consider any dilution of shareholders' interests. As it happens, Zedcor issued 6.9% more new shares over the last year. Therefore, each share now receives a smaller portion of profit. Per share metrics like EPS help us understand how much actual shareholders are benefitting from the company's profits, while the net income level gives us a better view of the company's absolute size. Check out Zedcor's historical EPS growth by clicking on this link.

How Is Dilution Impacting Zedcor's Earnings Per Share (EPS)?

Zedcor's net profit dropped by 54% per year over the last three years. On the bright side, in the last twelve months it grew profit by 67%. But EPS was less impressive, up only 40% in that time. So you can see that the dilution has had a bit of an impact on shareholders.

Changes in the share price do tend to reflect changes in earnings per share, in the long run. So Zedcor shareholders will want to see that EPS figure continue to increase. But on the other hand, we'd be far less excited to learn profit (but not EPS) was improving. For the ordinary retail shareholder, EPS is a great measure to check your hypothetical "share" of the company's profit.

Our Take On Zedcor's Profit Performance

As it turns out, Zedcor couldn't match its profit with cashflow and its dilution means that earnings per share growth is lagging net income growth. Considering all this we'd argue Zedcor's profits probably give an overly generous impression of its sustainable level of profitability. If you want to do dive deeper into Zedcor, you'd also look into what risks it is currently facing. In terms of investment risks, we've identified 2 warning signs with Zedcor, and understanding these should be part of your investment process.
